result: I keep coming back to the idea that every object has a secret life. My coffee mug, chipped on one side, remembers the frantic mornings when it was the only witness to the silent bargain I struck with dawn: five more minutes, then I’ll be human again. The wooden table bears the ghost of every spilled ink blot, each a failed constellation I tried to map when words refused to line up like good soldiers. Even the air feels crowded—full of exhalations from yesterday’s arguments, the soft sigh my mother released when I said I wasn’t coming home, the sharp intake before my niece laughed for the first time at the absurdity of bubbles. Sometimes I press my palm against the wall and swear it pulses, a slow heartbeat borrowed from everyone who’s ever leaned there, weary, triumphant, or just waiting for the kettle to boil. We think we’re surrounded by things, but we’re actually surrounded by stories wearing disguises. The umbrella isn’t nylon and metal; it’s a promise that tomorrow’s rain will not be the day we dissolve. The left shoe, perpetually loosening its lace, is a dare to trip, look up, notice the sky performing last-minute rehearsals for sunset. I’m learning to listen to these quiet narratives before they fray. When the power goes out and the devices fall dark and mute, the room sighs with relief: finally, an audience attentive enough to hear the furniture speak fluent memory.

Sometimes I press my palm against the wall and swear it pulses, a slow heartbeat borrowed from everyone who’s ever leaned there, weary, triumphant, or just waiting for the kettle to boil.
